title: SGD & Momentum Methods

learningObjectives.description: State the SGD with momentum update rule and explain how the velocity term damps oscillations across high-curvature directions
Distinguish Nesterov momentum from classical momentum and explain why evaluating the gradient at the lookahead position improves convergence
Apply weight decay in SGD as L2 regularization and state why L2 regularization and weight decay are equivalent for SGD but not for adaptive optimizers
Distinguish ASGD (parameter averaging for variance reduction) from Rprop (sign-based per-parameter step sizes) and identify when each is appropriate

content:
## Stochastic Gradient Descent

At its core, SGD replaces the full-dataset gradient with a mini-batch estimate:
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta \, g_t, \quad g_t = \frac{1}{B} \sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}} \nabla_{\theta} \ell_i$$

The noise from sampling is not purely harmful — it acts as implicit regularization and helps escape sharp minima. But vanilla SGD converges slowly on ill-conditioned loss surfaces because the optimal step size varies across directions.

```python
optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.01)
```

**TensorFlow:**
```python
optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.SGD(learning_rate=0.01)
```

## Momentum

Momentum adds a velocity term $v$ that accumulates gradients exponentially, smoothing oscillations across high-curvature directions:
$$v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + g_t$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta v_{t+1}$$

With $\beta = 0.9$, the effective gradient is a 10-step exponential moving average. This damps oscillations perpendicular to the optimum and accelerates along consistent gradient directions.

```python
optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(model.parameters(), lr=0.01, momentum=0.9)
```

**TensorFlow:**
```python
optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.SGD(learning_rate=0.01, momentum=0.9)
```

**Dampening** (`dampening > 0`) reduces the contribution of the current gradient to the velocity — useful when the gradient is noisy:
$$v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + (1 - \text{dampening}) \cdot g_t$$

## Nesterov Accelerated Gradient

Nesterov momentum evaluates the gradient at a *lookahead* position — where we would be after applying the current velocity — rather than at the current parameters:
$$\theta_{\text{look}} = \theta_t - \eta \beta v_t$$

$$v_{t+1} = \beta v_t + \nabla L(\theta_{\text{look}})$$

$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta v_{t+1}$$

This makes momentum *anticipatory* rather than *corrective*. Nesterov consistently outperforms classical momentum on convex problems and is widely used in practice:

```python
optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(
    model.parameters(), lr=0.01, momentum=0.9, nesterov=True
)
```

**TensorFlow:**
```python
optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.SGD(learning_rate=0.01, momentum=0.9, nesterov=True)
```

> **Note:** `nesterov=True` requires `momentum > 0` and `dampening = 0`.

## Weight Decay in SGD

SGD applies L2 regularization by adding $\lambda \theta$ to the gradient before the update:
$$\theta_{t+1} = \theta_t - \eta (g_t + \lambda \theta_t)$$

For SGD, L2 regularization and weight decay are equivalent. This equivalence breaks for adaptive optimizers (see AdamW).

```python
optimizer = torch.optim.SGD(
    model.parameters(), lr=0.01, momentum=0.9, weight_decay=1e-4
)
```

**TensorFlow:**
```python
# Keras SGD does not have weight_decay; apply via kernel_regularizer on each layer
# or use the newer tf.keras.optimizers.SGD with weight_decay (Keras 3 / TF 2.13+):
optimizer = tf.keras.optimizers.SGD(learning_rate=0.01, momentum=0.9, weight_decay=1e-4)
```

## ASGD — Averaged SGD

ASGD runs standard SGD but maintains a running average of parameter iterates, activated after `t0` steps:
$$\bar{\theta}_t = \frac{1}{t - t_0} \sum_{i=t_0}^{t} \theta_i$$

Averaging reduces variance from late-stage gradient noise. It's theoretically optimal for convex problems and occasionally used in NLP (classic LSTM training).

```python
optimizer = torch.optim.ASGD(
    model.parameters(), lr=0.01, lambd=1e-4, alpha=0.75, t0=1e6
)
# Use optimizer.state[p]['ax'] for the averaged parameters
```

**TensorFlow:** No built-in ASGD. Use `tf.train.experimental.enable_mixed_precision_graph_rewrite` or implement parameter averaging manually with `tf.train.ExponentialMovingAverage`:
```python
ema = tf.train.ExponentialMovingAverage(decay=0.999)
ema.apply(model.trainable_variables)  # call after each optimizer step
# For inference: use ema.average(var) instead of var
```

The `lambd` parameter adds a small per-step decay to the effective learning rate; `alpha` controls how fast that decay accelerates.

## Rprop — Resilient Backpropagation

Rprop ignores gradient magnitude entirely and updates each parameter by a fixed step size whose sign matches the gradient sign. Step sizes grow or shrink based on whether the gradient sign is consistent across consecutive steps:

- If `sign(g_t) == sign(g_{t-1})`: increase step size by `etas[1]` (default 1.2)
- If `sign(g_t) != sign(g_{t-1})`: decrease step size by `etas[0]` (default 0.5)
- Step sizes are clipped to `step_sizes` bounds (default 1e-6 to 50)

```python
optimizer = torch.optim.Rprop(
    model.parameters(), lr=0.01, etas=(0.5, 1.2), step_sizes=(1e-6, 50)
)
```

**TensorFlow:** No built-in Rprop. RMSprop (`tf.keras.optimizers.RMSprop`) is Rprop's direct descendant and is the practical TF substitute for mini-batch settings.

Rprop is effectively RMSprop's ancestor. It works well for full-batch training and small networks but poorly for mini-batch settings where sign changes are noisy.

## Choosing Between SGD Variants

| Variant | Best for |
|---|---|
| SGD (no momentum) | Convex baselines, simple experiments |
| SGD + momentum | Most supervised deep learning |
| SGD + Nesterov | When classical momentum shows oscillations |
| ASGD | Convex/quasi-convex language models, final-phase averaging |
| Rprop | Full-batch training, small networks |

For large-scale vision models (ResNets, ViTs), well-tuned SGD with momentum + cosine LR schedule often matches or beats Adam.

terms.name: Updated parameters
terms.desc: Parameter vector after applying this step's update

terms.name: Current parameters
terms.desc: Parameter vector before this step

terms.name: Learning rate
terms.desc: Global step size — too large causes divergence, too small causes slow convergence

terms.name: Mini-batch gradient
terms.desc: Average gradient over the current mini-batch: $\frac{1}{B}\sum_{i \in \mathcal{B}} \nabla_{\theta} \ell_i$

terms.name: Batch size
terms.desc: Number of samples in the mini-batch — larger B reduces gradient variance but increases memory

terms.name: Mini-batch indices
terms.desc: Random subset of training indices sampled each step

terms.name: Per-sample gradient
terms.desc: Gradient of the loss for a single example $i$ with respect to all parameters $\theta$

terms.name: New velocity
terms.desc: Exponentially weighted accumulation of past gradients — carries history from all previous steps

terms.name: Momentum coefficient
terms.desc: Decay factor for past velocity — typical value 0.9 gives roughly a 10-step exponential moving average

terms.name: Previous velocity
terms.desc: Accumulated gradient direction from the prior step

terms.name: Current gradient
terms.desc: Mini-batch gradient at step $t$ — added directly (no scaling) to the velocity

terms.name: Updated parameters
terms.desc: Parameters after moving in the direction of accumulated velocity

terms.name: Current parameters
terms.desc: Parameters before this step

terms.name: Learning rate
terms.desc: Scales the velocity vector before it is subtracted from the parameters

terms.name: Velocity
terms.desc: The smoothed gradient direction computed in the velocity update above

terms.name: Lookahead position
terms.desc: Where the parameters would be after applying the current velocity — the gradient is evaluated here, not at $\theta_t$

terms.name: Current parameters
terms.desc: Actual parameter values before the update

terms.name: Learning rate
terms.desc: Step size scalar

terms.name: Momentum coefficient
terms.desc: Controls how far ahead to look — larger β projects further along the current velocity direction

terms.name: Current velocity
terms.desc: Accumulated gradient direction from prior steps — defines the lookahead direction

terms.name: New velocity
terms.desc: Updated velocity incorporating the anticipatory gradient

terms.name: Momentum term
terms.desc: Decayed previous velocity — same as classical momentum

terms.name: Lookahead gradient
terms.desc: Gradient evaluated at the projected position $\theta_{\text{look}}$ — more informative than the gradient at $\theta_t$ because it anticipates the effect of the velocity step

terms.name: Updated parameters
terms.desc: Parameters after applying the corrected velocity

terms.name: Learning rate
terms.desc: Scales the velocity before subtracting from parameters

terms.name: Corrected velocity
terms.desc: Velocity built from the lookahead gradient — corrects for overshooting before it happens

terms.name: Updated parameters
terms.desc: Parameters after the regularized gradient step

terms.name: Current parameters
terms.desc: Parameters before this step — also appear in the penalty term

terms.name: Learning rate
terms.desc: Step size applied to the full augmented gradient

terms.name: Mini-batch gradient
terms.desc: Gradient of the loss with respect to parameters — the task signal

terms.name: Weight decay coefficient
terms.desc: Controls the strength of L2 regularization — typical values 1e-4 to 1e-2

terms.name: L2 penalty gradient
terms.desc: Gradient of $\frac{\lambda}{2}\|\theta\|^2$ — pulls parameters toward zero proportionally to their magnitude. For SGD, this is exactly equivalent to weight decay.

terms.name: Averaged parameters
terms.desc: Running average of all parameter iterates since $t_0$ — used for inference instead of $\theta_t$

terms.name: Number of averaged iterates
terms.desc: How many steps have been included in the average — grows over time, progressively reducing variance

terms.name: Averaging start step
terms.desc: Step at which averaging activates — SGD runs normally before this. PyTorch default: 1e6 (very late); set lower to enable averaging earlier

terms.name: Sum of iterates
terms.desc: Cumulative sum of all parameter vectors from step $t_0$ onward — dividing by the count gives the mean

terms.name: Iterate at step $i$
terms.desc: The actual SGD parameter vector at step $i$ — noisy due to mini-batch gradients, but the average cancels noise over time

terms.name: New velocity
terms.desc: Updated velocity with reduced gradient contribution

terms.name: Momentum coefficient
terms.desc: Decay factor applied to the previous velocity — unchanged by dampening

terms.name: Previous velocity
terms.desc: Accumulated gradient direction carried forward from the prior step

terms.name: Gradient scaling factor
terms.desc: Reduces how much of the current gradient is added — `dampening=0` recovers the standard momentum update; `dampening=0.5` halves the gradient's contribution

terms.name: Current gradient
terms.desc: Mini-batch gradient at step $t$, scaled down before accumulation when dampening > 0

references.label: Sutskever et al. 2013
references.title: On the importance of initialization and momentum in deep learning
references.note: Establishes Nesterov momentum as key for training deep networks

references.label: Polyak & Juditsky 1992
references.title: Acceleration of stochastic approximation by averaging
references.note: Original ASGD paper — iterate averaging theory
